behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is deterministic cryptographic logic. A unit test with mocked `crypto.verify()` and queue can verify: (1) invalid signatures trigger 401 rejection, (2) queue.push() is never called. Mocking crypto is safe because SHA256 is deterministic. Real failure: code skips validation or validates incorrectly. Unit test catches this.
behaviors.test_description: Send webhook with tampered body (signature no longer matches). Verify handler returns 401 status and does not enqueue the event.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This tests durability + ordering. A unit test with mocked queue can verify call order (queue.push called before res.send), but cannot verify the event actually persisted. Real failure: event is lost because queue write failed or was async without acknowledgment. Only a real (test) queue with persistent backing (SQLite, Redis, or in-memory persistent store) can catch this. Mocking persistence hides the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Send valid webhook to handler. Query the persistent queue directly and verify the event exists. Capture the timing of the HTTP response. Assert that the event was persisted before the 200 response was sent.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: This tests async behavior (non-blocking response). Real failure: code has `await downstream()` before `res.send(200)`, blocking the response. A unit test catches this by: (1) mocking downstream processing with a slow/long-running mock, (2) measuring that HTTP 200 is returned before the mock completes. Mocking downstream is safe because we're testing handler logic, not downstream reliability.
behaviors.test_description: Mock downstream processing to take 5+ seconds. Send webhook to handler. Verify HTTP 200 response is returned within milliseconds, before downstream processing completes.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This tests response latency SLA (< 3s) under load. Response time = signature validation + queue persistence + downstream (async). Unit test with instant mocked queue would always pass. Real failure: queue persistence adds 2+ seconds of latency (network, disk), exceeding 3s SLA. Only an integration test with a real queue (or realistic latency-bearing queue mock) catches this. Without testing actual queue latency, slow queue failures slip through.
behaviors.test_description: Send multiple concurrent webhook requests while mocking slow downstream processing (10+ seconds). Measure wall-clock response time for each request. Verify all responses arrive within 3 seconds, even while earlier events are processing.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: This tests idempotency / deduplication. A unit test with in-memory dedup state would pass, but real failure: process restart loses in-memory dedup state, and duplicate event is enqueued (if dedup is not persisted). Or: dedup state is persisted but to volatile storage. Real failure boundary: dedup state must survive process restarts. Only integration test with a persistent dedup store (database, queue metadata, or Redis) catches this.
behaviors.test_description: Send two webhooks with the same event ID (at different times). Verify that only the first event is queued. The second webhook should be rejected or acknowledged without queueing (using a persistent queue that maintains dedup state).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This tests error handling logic. A unit test with mocked queue returning a 'full' status can verify: (1) handler checks queue capacity, (2) returns 503 status, (3) sets Retry-After header. Real failure: code doesn't check queue.isFull() or doesn't set Retry-After. Unit test with deterministic mocks catches this logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the queue to report it is full (or throw a capacity-exceeded error). Send webhook to handler. Verify handler returns 503 status code with a Retry-After header set.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue implementation itself, not the handler. A unit test with mocked queue cannot verify that a real queue enforces capacity. Real failure: queue capacity limit is not enforced (e.g., queue allows more events than configured). Only an integration test against a real queue with real capacity limits catches this. Must use actual queue (SQLite, Redis, RabbitMQ, etc.) to verify enforcement.
behaviors.test_description: Fill a real queue to its configured capacity limit. Attempt to add one more event. Verify the addition is rejected or returns an error indicating the queue is full.
